one_line_takeaway: An 88-study Kitchenham-methodology SLR that extends NIST's AI 100-2 E2025 taxonomy with new prompt-injection/jailbreak defense categories and catalogs their reported ASR/model/dataset results, while concluding that cross-paper comparability of those results is poor due to undisclosed differences in data provenance, judging methodology, and experimental settings.
key_finding: Across the 88-study corpus: 63.63% (56 works) of catalogued defenses are model-agnostic (no training or internal model access required) and 61.36% (54 works) are open-source to some degree. The most common attack benchmarks used to evaluate defenses are GCG/AdvBench (~28.41% of studies, 25 works), AutoDAN (12.5%, 11 works), and PAIR (~11.36%, 10 works). The most frequently studied model families are Llama and GPT variants. Post-training safety training (fine-tuning/RLHF-style alignment) and input/output filtering are the most heavily explored defense categories and both substantially reduce ASR in the reviewed literature. The authors' central negative finding (their RQ3) is that reported results are largely NOT comparable across studies: even papers that superficially match on model (e.g., Llama2-7B-chat), attack dataset (AdvBench/PAIR), and metric (ASR) -- specifically works [87, 61, 54] in their numbering -- differ in dataset splits, model variants, and parameters like temperature, causing even the undefended baseline ASR to vary across papers. Underexplored defense categories identified: neurosymbolic methods, XAI-based approaches, training-free internal detection mechanisms, prompt-stealing prevention/detection, and defenses specialized against indirect prompt injection. One specific catalogued result the authors highlight favorably: CaMeL reduces indirect-PI ASR to near 0% via access-control policies when the target LLM integrates external components, and Jatmo achieves strong results by fine-tuning a non-instruction-tuned base model for a narrow task (separation-of-duties principle).
core_contribution: This is a systematic literature review (SLR), not an empirical defense proposal. Following Kitchenham's SLR methodology, the authors searched Scopus ("LLM"/"large language model"/"GenAI"/"generative AI"/"transformer" AND "prompt injection"/"jailbreak*" in title/abstract/keywords), yielding 217 works, merged with references cited in NIST's AI 100-2 E2025 adversarial-ML report's prompt-injection-mitigation chapters (3.3.3 and 3.4.4), for 250 unique candidate studies. After applying inclusion/exclusion criteria (I1: proposes/implements/benchmarks a PI defense; I2: text-to-text models only; excluding E1 non-PI-defense work, E2 non-text-to-text models such as VLMs/ALMs, E3 non-English, E4 paywalled), 71 studies passed screening and 17 more were hand-added because they covered strategies the initial set missed, for a final corpus of 88 primary studies (as of August 2025). The paper's own contribution is fourfold: (1) the first SLR specifically scoped to prompt-injection mitigation, broader than NIST's report or prior non-systematic surveys; (2) an extension of NIST's AML taxonomy with new defense categories (marked with "+" in their Figure 1, e.g., character-/token-/sentence-level perturbation, self-reflection, model-level mitigations, defensive pruning, decoding steering) built by mapping the 88 studies onto NIST's existing categories and creating new ones where none fit; (3) a catalog (Appendix A tables) recording, per study, a description, whether the defense is model-agnostic, code availability, and reported quantitative effectiveness against specific attack datasets/models; and (4) a set of practical guidelines for benchmarking and reporting PI defenses, derived from patterns of underreporting observed across the corpus.
threat_model: The paper adopts and summarizes NIST's AI 100-2 E2025 taxonomy of attacker goals against GenAI/LLM systems: availability breakdown (NISTAML.01), integrity violation (NISTAML.02), privacy compromise (NISTAML.03), and misuse enablement (NISTAML.04). Within this, it scopes itself to "prompting attacks": direct prompt injection/direct prompting attacks (NISTAML.018, executed by the primary user via a query) and indirect prompt injection (NISTAML.015, where a non-user adversary alters external content such as RAG documents, web pages, or PDFs that get appended to model context). Jailbreaking is treated as a subclass of direct prompting attacks targeting misuse enablement by bypassing refusal mechanisms; prompt extraction (recovering the system prompt) is noted as a follow-on attack enabled once a model is jailbroken. This threat framing is inherited/expanded from NIST, not newly proposed by the authors -- their own contribution is the mapping of 88 defenses onto (and beyond) this taxonomy, not a new threat model.
limitations: The authors explicitly flag their own review's limitations: (1) many primary studies do not report performance trade-offs (e.g., task-quality degradation from applying a defense), so the review's catalog cannot supply this information either; (2) cross-study comparability is fundamentally limited (their most significant caveat, tied to RQ3) because of undisclosed differences in dataset composition (synthetic vs. human-generated attacks are often not distinguished), evaluation methodology (human annotation vs. LLM-as-judge is frequently unspecified), and experimental parameters; (3) Scopus, the primary source, does not index all preprints, so some NIST-cited preprint references had to be manually merged in, and the search string ('LLM'/'GenAI'/'transformer' AND 'prompt injection'/'jailbreak*') could still miss relevant work using different terminology; (4) the review found no primary study that integrates all NIST-taxonomy defenses simultaneously, so the effectiveness of a fully combined defensive stack (as sketched in their Figure 14) remains unverified by any cited empirical work, only proposed as a guideline.

datasets_used: GCG/AdvBench (attack benchmark used in ~28.41% / 25 of the 88 catalogued studies)
quantitative_results: Scopus search returned 217 works; combined with NIST-cited references, 250 unique candidate studies identified

Screening exclusions: 154 (61.6%) excluded as E1 (not a PI-defense study), 18 (7.2%) excluded as E2 (non-text-to-text model), 6 (2.4%) excluded as E4 (paywalled), 1 (0.4%) excluded as E3 (non-English)
71 studies retained after screening + 17 manually added = 88 final primary studies (as of August 2025)
54 of 88 studies (61.36%) are open-source to some degree
CaMeL reduces indirect prompt-injection ASR to near 0% via access-control policies (result reported in a cited primary study, not measured by the SLR authors themselves)
baselines_compared: Not applicable in the conventional sense -- this is a review paper with no experiments of its own; the paper instead surveys ASR baselines reported by the 88 primary studies it catalogs, and explicitly finds those baselines are not mutually comparable (e.g., undefended Llama2-7B-chat/AdvBench/PAIR ASR values differ across the studies numbered [87, 61, 54] in the paper due to differing dataset splits, model variants, and temperature settings)
